description: Theory-grounded enumerations for genre classification, narrative collision types, antagonist typology, intertextuality, irony, speech acts, gaze, and other Grimoire-specific literary-theoretical fields.

genre_type.description: Top-level genre classification for the narrative frame.

values.definition: Prioritizes psychological depth, thematic complexity, and style over genre plot conventions.
Organized primarily around plot engagement, reader pleasure, and genre conventions.
Central conflict organized around the formation or maintenance of a romantic relationship; mandatory HEA or HFN.
High-stakes suspense narrative with external physical or institutional threat.
Organized around the discovery and resolution of a crime or puzzle; epistemological drive.
Organized around the encounter with a monstrous or threatening entity; Noël Carroll's art-horror (fear + disgust).
Novum-based fiction: a scientifically conceivable difference from consensus reality drives the narrative (Darko Suvin's cognitive estrangement).
Narrative in a secondary world or with supernatural elements not explained by scientific logic.
Set in a documented historical period; Lukács/Hutcheon — the relationship between past and present is a central concern.
Set in the present or recent past without generic overlay.
Hybrid: literary-quality writing with commercial accessibility and plot momentum.
Organized around criminal acts and their investigation, prosecution, or commission.
Umbrella for SF, fantasy, horror, and their hybrids — 'what if' fiction of any kind.
Autobiographical narrative focused on a thematic slice of the author's life.
Hybrid of autobiography and fiction; Doubrovsky/Lejeune — the author's name appears but the fictionality is signaled.
Formally innovative work that foregrounds its own construction and departs from conventions of realism.
Primary audience is adolescent readers; typically features a teenage protagonist and coming-of-age or power negotiation themes (Trites).
Primary audience is children ages 8–12; deals with friendship, identity, and discovery within more protected moral frameworks.
Primarily visual narrative for young children; McCloud/Chute interdependence of image and text.
Sequential art narrative; McCloud's closure and gutter; panel transitions as primary narrative device.
Novel-length narrative told in verse; prosody carries semantic and structural weight.
A work that deliberately combines two or more genre or form categories; the hybrid nature is a defining rather than accidental feature.

collision_type.description: The primary structural collision pattern — the fundamental way the story's central conflict is organized. Grimoire-specific.

values.definition: The protagonist's central conflict is with another specific individual who opposes their want.
The protagonist's central conflict is internal — with their own beliefs, wounds, or psychology.
The protagonist's central conflict is with social norms, institutions, or collective power.
The protagonist's central conflict is with the natural environment or physical forces.
The protagonist's central conflict is with destiny, prophecy, or forces beyond any agent's control.
The protagonist's central conflict involves technological systems or AI that oppose or constrain them.
The protagonist's central conflict involves forces outside the natural order — gods, monsters, curses.
Two communities, factions, or social groups are in central conflict; individual characters enact that group collision.
Two philosophical positions or value systems are in collision; characters embody competing ideas.

antagonist_type.description: The typological category of the story's primary opposing force.

values.definition: A specific individual character who opposes the protagonist's want.
values.example_usage: A competing character; a villain.

values.definition: An organization, system, or institution that opposes the protagonist.
values.example_usage: A government, a corporation, a church.

values.definition: The physical environment or natural forces as the primary opposition.
values.example_usage: A storm, a disease, a hostile landscape.

values.definition: The protagonist's own psychology, wound, or misbelief as the primary opposing force.
values.example_usage: Internal arc where the protagonist's greatest enemy is themselves.

values.definition: Social norms, collective prejudice, or cultural expectations as the primary opposition.
values.example_usage: A protagonist fighting against what 'everyone knows' is acceptable.

values.definition: A supernatural entity, force, or curse as the primary opposition.
values.example_usage: A monster, a curse, a god, a supernatural system.

values.definition: A technological system, AI, or technological process as the primary opposition.
values.example_usage: An AI that opposes the protagonist; a system that has turned hostile.

values.definition: No specific agent — the collision is with destiny, chance, or unavoidable circumstance.
values.example_usage: Terminal illness; an inevitable historical event; inescapable tragedy.

opposition_level.description: The intensity of active opposition the antagonist force is currently applying (Truby's five opposition levels).

values.definition: The opposing force creates minor obstacles without direct targeting of the protagonist's crucial goal.
values.example_usage: Early antagonist behavior; inconveniences; obstacles not yet aimed at core want.

values.definition: The opposing force actively contests the protagonist's goal in specific scenes.
values.example_usage: A confrontation; a competition; a direct dispute.

values.definition: The opposing force develops a deliberate strategy specifically aimed at defeating the protagonist.
values.example_usage: The antagonist begins planning against the protagonist specifically.

values.definition: The protagonist discovers the antagonist's counter-plan; the full scope of opposition is revealed.
values.example_usage: A revelation scene that escalates the threat level.

values.definition: The opposing force makes an all-out attempt to destroy or decisively defeat the protagonist.
values.example_usage: Climax-level confrontation; the final assault.

transtextuality_type.description: Genette's five transtextual relations — how this text relates to other texts.

values.definition: Direct text-to-text reference in the form of quotation, allusion, or plagiarism.
values.example_usage: A title that quotes another text; a direct allusion in the prose.

values.definition: Relation between the text and its paratexts — titles, epigraphs, dedications, prefaces, notes (Genette, Seuils).
values.example_usage: An epigraph that reframes the novel; a title that creates ironic distance.

values.definition: Commentary relation: this text discusses but does not cite the other text.
values.example_usage: A novel that critiques or interprets another without directly quoting it.

values.definition: Transformation relation: this text derives from an earlier text (hypertext) through transformation or imitation.
values.example_usage: A retelling; a parody; a sequel; a Bildungsroman reworking.

values.definition: The generic contract: the relation between this text and its genres, modes, and discourse types.
values.example_usage: The implicit genre frame a text invites the reader to apply.

irony_type.description: Booth/Hutcheon taxonomy of irony types present in the narrative.

values.definition: Speaker's literal meaning differs from intended meaning; the real meaning is fixed and recoverable (Booth).
values.example_usage: Classic sarcasm; 'How charming.' said of something repellent.

values.definition: Speaker's meaning is indeterminate; the gap between literal and implied meaning cannot be fully closed.
values.example_usage: Ambiguous free indirect discourse; unreliable irony.

values.definition: The reader knows more than one or more characters; the character's unknowing actions carry ironic weight.
values.example_usage: Oedipus; a character walking into a trap the reader can see.

values.definition: Outcomes are contrary to what was expected, intended, or would be considered appropriate.
values.example_usage: The doctor dies of the disease they treat; the safety precaution causes the accident.

values.definition: The overall form, ending, or architecture of the work undercuts or reverses the surface meaning.
values.example_usage: A novel framed as a romance that ends in ironic non-resolution.

values.definition: Self-reflexive authorial irony: the text calls attention to its own constructedness, undermining the illusion of fiction (Schlegel).
values.example_usage: An author-intrusion that breaks the fourth wall; postmodern self-reference.

values.definition: Deliberate feigned ignorance to expose the other's actual ignorance or contradiction.
values.example_usage: A character who pretends not to know what they know to force a revelation.

gaze_type.description: The type of looking relationship encoded in the scene — who looks, at whom, with what power implications (Mulvey, Foucault, Sartre).

values.definition: The focalizer's perspective treats another character as a visual object of desire, enacting the power structure Mulvey identifies in classical Hollywood cinema.
values.example_usage: A male focalizer's lingering description of a female character's body as spectacle.

values.definition: The looked-at character returns the gaze, subverting or refusing objectification.
values.example_usage: A character who stares back and is not diminished by being looked at.

values.definition: A character is observed by an institutional or anonymous gaze they cannot return; produces self-surveillance (Foucault).
values.example_usage: A prisoner aware of potential surveillance; a character in a monitored space.

values.definition: The focalizer looks at another character with the intention of understanding rather than possession or judgment.
values.example_usage: A POV character who attempts to genuinely see another's experience from inside it.

values.definition: The focalizer looks at another character as a specimen — analytically, without empathy.
values.example_usage: A scientist, detective, or diagnostician regarding another as a case to be solved.

values.definition: A character turns the gaze on themselves — introspection, self-assessment, self-judgment.
values.example_usage: A character who examines their own reflection, literally or metaphorically.

speech_act_category.description: Searle's five illocutionary act categories — the performative function of what is said in dialogue.

values.definition: The speaker commits to the truth of a proposition — claims, states, describes, asserts, predicts.
values.example_usage: Any statement that can be evaluated as true or false.

values.definition: The speaker tries to get the listener to do something — orders, requests, questions, begs, challenges.
values.example_usage: Commands; questions (which direct the listener to answer); requests.

values.definition: The speaker commits to a future action — promises, vows, threatens, offers.
values.example_usage: A promise; a threat; a pledge; a bid.

values.definition: The speaker expresses a psychological state — thanks, apologizes, congratulates, condoles, laments.
values.example_usage: An apology; a declaration of love; an expression of grief.

values.definition: The speaker changes institutional reality through the speech act itself — pronounces, fires, sentences, names, declares.
values.example_usage: 'I now pronounce you married.' 'You're fired.' 'I hereby sentence you.'

freudian_mechanism.description: Psychoanalytic defense mechanisms encoded in character behavior or narrative structure.

values.definition: Unconsciously excluding threatening thoughts, feelings, or memories from conscious awareness.
values.example_usage: A character who cannot remember a traumatic event; a narrator who 'forgets' key details.

values.definition: Multiple unconscious ideas fused into a single image, figure, or scene (Freud, dream-work).
values.example_usage: A dream figure who combines several important people; a symbol carrying overdetermined meaning.

values.definition: Shifting emotional investment from its real object to a substitute (Freud, dream-work).
values.example_usage: A character who directs anger at the wrong person; a phobia that stands in for a deeper fear.

values.definition: Attributing one's own unacceptable qualities to others.
values.example_usage: A character who accuses others of what they themselves are doing.

values.definition: Channeling socially unacceptable impulses into acceptable activities.
values.example_usage: An artist whose creativity is routed aggression; a scientist whose cold precision is routed emotion.

values.definition: Constructing post-hoc logical justifications for actions driven by unconscious motives.
values.example_usage: A narrator who explains their destructive choices in rational terms that don't hold up.

values.definition: Refusing to accept an evident reality.
values.example_usage: A character who cannot process a loss and acts as if it hasn't happened.

values.definition: Repressed material returning in disguised, symptomatic, or compulsive form.
values.example_usage: The uncanny; compulsive repetition of a traumatic pattern.
